title: Circular Queue
description: A fixed-size array queue that wraps around using modulo -- the array-based cousin of the Circular Linked List, solving a real space-waste problem.
objectives: Understand the wasted-space problem a naive array queue has
Implement a circular queue using front/rear indices and modulo wraparound
Distinguish a full circular queue from an empty one

sections.text: 1. The Problem: A Naive Array Queue Wastes Space

After several enqueue/dequeue operations, front and rear only ever move FORWARD -- once rear reaches the array's end, there's no way to reuse the empty space at the beginning left behind by earlier dequeues, even though it's genuinely free. A circular queue fixes this by wrapping indices back to 0 using modulo, exactly like the Circular Linked List wrapped its LAST node back to the first.
A fixed-size array-based queue where the front and rear indices wrap around to the beginning using modulo arithmetic once they reach the end, reusing freed space instead of wasting it.

sections.title: Tracing the wraparound
steps: dequeue (returns 10): front becomes (0+1)%5=1, size=2. Array unchanged, just front moved: [_,20,30,_,_]
enqueue 60: rear=(2+3)%5=0 -- WRAPS AROUND to index 0! array[0]=60, size=4: [60,_,30,40,50]
Reading in logical order from front=2, size=4: queue[2]=30, queue[3]=40, queue[4]=50, queue[(2+3)%5=0]=60 -> 30 40 50 60

sections.text: Notice index 0 now holds 60, even though logically 60 is the LAST element in the queue -- the physical array position and the logical queue order are no longer the same thing. This is exactly why reading the queue requires (front + i) % queue.length, not a plain 0-to-size loop over the raw array.

A naive circular queue using ONLY front and rear indices (no separate size counter) has a classic ambiguity: front == rear could mean either completely empty OR completely full, since rear wraps back around to meet front in both cases. Tracking size explicitly (as done above) sidesteps this problem entirely -- some implementations instead sacrifice one array slot to disambiguate the two cases without a size counter.
4. Why Use a Circular Queue Instead of ArrayDeque?

In practice, java.util.ArrayDeque (from Queue and Deque) already handles this wraparound internally and is what you'd use in real code. Building a circular queue manually here is purely educational -- to understand the mechanics ArrayDeque hides, similar to how Manual Heap Implementation explained what PriorityQueue does internally.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting the modulo when computing rear or advancing front, causing an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException once the index would naturally exceed the array's length.
Using front == rear alone to detect "empty" when the queue could also be completely full at that exact same condition -- track size explicitly, or sacrifice one slot.
Reading the queue's contents with a plain 0-to-size loop over the raw array instead of (front + i) % length -- this ignores that the logical front might not be at physical index 0.
Forgetting to check size == queue.length before enqueuing, silently overwriting data that hasn't been dequeued yet.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What problem does a circular queue solve compared to a naive array-based queue?
questions.answer: A naive array queue only ever advances front and rear forward, wasting the space freed by earlier dequeues once rear reaches the array's end. A circular queue wraps both indices back to 0 via modulo, reusing that freed space.

questions.question: Why is front == rear ambiguous in a circular queue implemented without a separate size counter?
questions.answer: Both a completely empty queue and a completely full queue can result in front and rear pointing to the same index, since rear wraps around to meet front in either case -- an explicit size counter (or sacrificing one array slot) resolves the ambiguity.

questions.question: Why must reading a circular queue's elements use (front + i) % length instead of a plain array loop?
questions.answer: Because the logical front of the queue may not be at physical array index 0 after wraparound has occurred -- the modulo maps the logical position back to wherever it actually lives in the underlying array.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A circular queue is a fixed-size array queue where front and rear wrap around to index 0 via modulo once they reach the array's end, reusing space freed by earlier dequeues instead of wasting it like a naive array queue would. A classic pitfall is that front == rear is ambiguous between empty and full without an explicit size counter, since rear wraps back to meet front in both cases. Reading the queue's contents requires (front + i) % length rather than a plain array loop, since the logical front may sit anywhere in the physical array after enough wraparounds. In real code, java.util.ArrayDeque already implements this wraparound internally.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Circular queue = a circular parking lot, not a dead-end street -- car #6 wraps around to spot #1 if it's now empty, instead of the lot pretending it's full when half the spaces are actually free.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What space-waste problem does a circular queue solve?
Why is front == rear ambiguous without tracking size separately?
Why must reading the queue use (front + i) % length instead of a plain loop?
What real Java class already implements this wraparound behavior internally?
